summary:
The Battle of Vouillé (507 AD) was a decisive engagement in which the [[event:2041|Frankish]] king Clovis I defeated and killed the Visigothic king Alaric II near Poitiers in modern France, ending Visigothic domination of southern Gaul and establishing the Franks as the dominant power in western Europe. Clovis led his Frankish forces south in what amounted to a religious war as well as a political one: Clovis had recently converted to Nicene (Catholic) Christianity, while the Visigoths were Arian Christians, and the Catholic hierarchy of Gaul actively supported the Frankish campaign, viewing it as a just war against heretics.

The battle's outcome was swift and total: Alaric II was killed (reportedly by Clovis personally), the Visigothic army routed, and Aquitaine — the wealthy southwestern region that had been the heart of the Visigothic kingdom in Gaul — passed to Frankish control. The Visigoths were pushed into Spain, where they established the [[event:2039|Visigothic Kingdom of Toledo]] that would last until the Arab conquest of 711. Battle of Vouillé established the basic political geography of western Europe for centuries: Frankish (and eventually French) control of Gaul, and Visigothic then later Spanish control of Iberia. The Franks' Catholic identity and their church alliance, cemented by their victory over the Arian Visigoths, reinforced the Frankish-papal relationship that would eventually culminate in Charlemagne's coronation as Holy Roman Emperor in 800 AD.
